theme: Every Pixel Should Pull Its Weight

ideas.title: Capacity Ratio — '1' Is Not a Number Without Context
ideas.description: The StatsBar 'ON THE CLOCK' card shows a raw integer (e.g., '1'). Without knowing team size, '1' is meaningless — is that everyone, or is it a crisis? The roster array is already fetched and passed into App state. Fix: pass roster as a prop to StatsBar (it already receives workers). Inside the 'total' stat card, add a sub-label: {total}/{roster.length} beneath the big number. CSS: .stat-capacity { font-size: 0.7rem; color: var(--text-muted); font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.02em; margin-top: 2px; display: block; }. When total === roster.length, add a 'full squad' badge: {total === roster.length && 🌕 full squad}. CSS: .stat-full-squad { font-size: 0.6rem; background: rgba(34,197,94,0.15); color: #4ade80; border-radius: 20px; padding: 1px 6px; }. WHY: Right now every single number in the stats bar is an island. '1/5 active' is a dashboard. '1' is a counter. The ratio transforms the stat from a count into a health signal — a manager scanning from across the room instantly knows team coverage. This also makes the full-squad celebration (🌕) feel earned because you can see the fraction climbing toward it.

ideas.title: Role-Color Card Border — Know Who's Who Before You Read
ideas.description: Worker cards currently have a uniform left border with status colors (green for working, etc.). Meanwhile, ROLE_COLORS is a rich map in constants.js — purple for Frontend, teal for Backend, orange for DevOps — and it's already used on the office floor via '--role-color'. The dashboard card ignores it entirely. Fix in WorkerCard: the card's outer div already gets 'worker' status as className. Add a CSS custom property for role: style={{ '--i': index, '--role-color': ROLE_COLORS[worker.role] ?? '#6b7280' }}. In App.css: .worker-card { border-left: 3px solid var(--role-color, var(--border)); }. Override for status states: .worker-card.working { border-left-color: var(--role-color, var(--green)); } .worker-card.error { border-left-color: var(--red); } (error always overrides to red). Add a 1px role-color tint to the avatar bg: .worker-avatar { background: color-mix(in srgb, var(--role-color, var(--accent)) 15%, transparent); border-radius: 10px; }. WHY: The office floor already uses role colors to colorize desks and character rings. The dashboard cards are visually disconnected from the same data. Luna = deep purple, Carlos = teal, Dave = orange. Color-as-identity is the fastest scannable signal in a busy grid. When you have 5+ workers, you stop reading names and start reading colors.

ideas.title: Vacant Desk Roster Preview — Empty Desks Should Whisper a Name
ideas.description:
When a desk is vacant (no working worker assigned to it), the desk renders a black monitor and '📋 Vacant' nameplate. The effectiveRoster array and ghostRoster are both available in Office.jsx. Ghost characters (OOO roster members) sit at some desks, but if there are more desks than ghost+working workers, the extras just say Vacant. Fix: calculate unassigned roster members who aren't ghosts and aren't working: const benchedWorkers = effectiveRoster.filter(r => r.id !== 'bloberto' && !activeIds.has(r.id) && !ghostRoster.includes(r)). Then in the desk render loop, after assigning working and ghost occupants, assign benched workers to remaining vacant desks. Render their nameplate as:
{worker.name.split(' ')[0]}
. CSS: .desk__nameplate--benched { color: var(--text-muted); opacity: 0.45; font-style: italic; font-size: 0.58rem; }. The monitor gets: .desk--benched .desk__monitor { opacity: 0.3; }. WHY: A blank 'Vacant' desk reads as 'this team is shrinking.' A dimmed, italicised name reads as 'this person is just not here yet.' Two different emotional signals. The second one is true: the team has a defined roster, and every desk belongs to someone. Making that visible — even faintly — gives the office the feeling of a real workplace, not a sparse prototype.

ideas.title: Whiteboard Live Counter — Make the Board Earn Its Space
ideas.description:
The office whiteboard renders a static vibe slogan (VIBE_WHITEBOARD[vibe]) — '🏆 SHIP IT', '⚡ FLOW', etc. It's reactive to vibe but totally disconnected from the actual numbers. The whiteboard is one of the few labeled, prominent props on the floor — it should earn its place. In Office.jsx, the whiteboard already receives props via the Office component (workers array). Compute: const workingCount = workers.filter(w => w.status === 'working').length. Change the whiteboard render to:
{VIBE_WHITEBOARD[vibe]}
{workingCount > 0 ? `${workingCount} active` : 'quiet here...'}
. CSS: .office-whiteboard__count { font-size: 0.45rem; color: rgba(255,255,255,0.45); margin-top: 2px; letter-spacing: 0.05em; text-transform: lowercase; font-style: italic; }. When vibe === 'on-fire', override to show error count instead: {hasErrors ? `${errorCount} BLOCKED` : ...}. WHY: The whiteboard is a physical metaphor for team status. Right now it says 'SHIP IT' while only one person is even at their desk — the board doesn't know. Two lines of live data on a whiteboard turn a decoration into an artifact. It's the kind of detail that makes people lean in on a demo.

ideas.title: Progress Bar In-Motion Stripes — Working Should Look Like Working
ideas.description: The ProgressBar component renders a solid-fill bar for all statuses. For a worker who is actively 'working', the bar looks identical to a paused or done bar — just a static colored rectangle. The fill already has a className hook: 'progress-bar-fill'. Add a working-state variant in App.jsx: the WorkerCard knows worker.status, and it already renders ProgressBar. Pass a prop: <ProgressBar progress={worker.progress} isWorking={worker.status === 'working'} />. In ProgressBar, add isWorking to className: className={['progress-bar-fill', progress >= 100 ? 'progress-bar-fill--overflow' : '', isWorking ? 'progress-bar-fill--active' : '', flashClass].join(' ')}. CSS: .progress-bar-fill--active { background: repeating-linear-gradient( 45deg, var(--accent), var(--accent) 6px, color-mix(in srgb, var(--accent) 70%, transparent) 6px, color-mix(in srgb, var(--accent) 70%, transparent) 12px ); background-size: 200% 100%; animation: bar-march 1.5s linear infinite; } @keyframes bar-march { 0% { background-position: 0 0; } 100% { background-position: 60px 0; } } @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .progress-bar-fill--active { animation: none; } }. WHY: A progress bar for someone actively working should feel like motion — not a frozen screenshot. The marching-stripe pattern is a classic 'in progress' indicator (OS installers, loading bars). Without it, 34% could mean 'working right now' or 'stuck since yesterday.' With it, you glance and know: something is happening in here.

ideas.title: Worker Card Clocked-In Time — Duration Needs an Anchor
ideas.description:
WorkerCard shows 'Working for 18m' via formatDuration(worker.updated_at). This tells you how long, but not when — and 'when' is context a manager actually needs. If it's 2pm and someone has been working for 18m, they came back from lunch. If it's 9:02am, they were first in. Implementation: add a 'clocked-in' line below the duration indicator that shows the start time. In WorkerCard, after the {worker.status === 'working' && worker.updated_at && ...} duration block, add: {worker.status === 'working' && worker.updated_at && (
🕐 Clocked in {new Date(worker.updated_at).toLocaleTimeString('en-US', { hour: 'numeric', minute: '2-digit', hour12: true })}
)}. CSS: .worker-clockin { font-size: 0.66rem; color: var(--text-muted); margin-top: -4px; margin-bottom: 4px; font-style: italic; letter-spacing: 0.01em; }. WHY: 'Working for 4m' tells you duration. 'Clocked in 8:04 AM' tells you a story. Right now every worker card is timeblind — you know how long they've been working but not when they started. The clock-in time anchors the card to the real workday, which is what a live dashboard is supposed to do. It also surfaces 'they started at 6am' or 'they only just came online' at a glance.

ideas.title: Keyboard Shortcuts Legend — Discoverable Power Is Still Power
ideas.description:
The app has 4 keyboard shortcuts: 1 (Office tab), 2 (Dashboard tab), R (refresh), Escape (blur). They exist, they work, and zero users know about them unless they read the title attributes. Add a '?' button to the header-right, between the sync button and theme toggle: . Add a useState: const [showShortcuts, setShowShortcuts] = useState(false). Add '?' to the keyboard handler: else if (e.key === '?') setShowShortcuts(s => !s). Render a small floating panel (not a full modal): {showShortcuts &&
Keyboard Shortcuts
{[['1', 'Office view'], ['2', 'Dashboard view'], ['R', 'Refresh data'], ['?', 'This panel'], ['Esc', 'Unfocus']].map(([key, label]) => (
{key}{label}
))}
}. CSS: .shortcuts-panel { position:fixed; bottom:1.5rem; right:1.5rem; background:var(--surface2); border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:12px; padding:1rem; z-index:200; min-width:200px; box-shadow:0 8px 32px rgba(0,0,0,0.3); animation:shortcut-in 0.2s ease both; } kbd { background:var(--surface3); border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:4px; padding:1px 6px; font-family:monospace; font-size:0.75rem; }. WHY: Undiscoverable shortcuts are just secrets. The moment a user finds '?' they feel like an insider — they go from tourist to power user. This panel costs 30 lines of code and permanently upgrades how people use the product. It's the kind of detail that shows up in every glowing Product Hunt comment.

ideas.title: Office Floor Break Zone — Give the Lower Third a Purpose
ideas.description: The office floor uses aspect-ratio: 4/3 in Office.css, which means the lower ~35% of the floor below the second desk row is empty dark grid. The screenshots confirm it: there's roughly 200px of empty floor below Dave and Sofia's row — desks end at ~67% height. This void reads as 'unfinished' to first-time visitors. Solution: add a designated Break Zone to the lower-left quadrant of the floor. In Office.jsx, add a static <div className='break-zone'> element to the office-floor. Contents: a couch SVG (simple path: two cushions, a back), a side table, and a 'BREAK ROOM' label. CSS: .break-zone { position: absolute; left: 4%; bottom: 8%; width: 28%; display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: flex-start; gap: 4px; } .break-zone__label { font-size: 0.4rem; color: var(--text-muted); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.12em; opacity: 0.5; }. When idleWorkers wander with wanderIdx === 4 ('🧘 Taking a mindful moment'), position them near this zone (left: 10%, bottom: 14%). The wander-4 CSS path can already be adjusted. WHY: An intentional break room transforms 'empty void' into 'we planned this.' Idle workers drifting toward a visible couch is exactly the kind of physical simulation detail that makes Bloberto's Office feel like a world, not a widget. It also gives the idle wander paths geographic meaning — they're going somewhere real.

ideas.title: Vibe-Reactive Plants — Living Decor That Tells the Story
ideas.description: The office floor has three plants (two leafy in Office.jsx at left:85%/left:3% and a cactus at left:92%). They're purely decorative SVGs that never change. The cactus has a bloom circle on top — it's already designed to show health state. In Office.jsx: pass vibe to the plant render area. Create a PlantHealth component that receives vibeKey and renders different states: when vibe === 'crushing', all plants get a subtle bright-green brightness and a small sparkle (add a <circle cx='10' cy='4' r='1' fill='#fbbf24' opacity='0.8' /> bloom at random leaf tips). When vibe === 'on-fire', leaves get slightly yellowed and drooped: <ellipse transform='rotate(8)' fill='#d97706' instead of #22c55e. When vibe === 'slow-day', add a subtle droop: the left cactus arm drops 2px. Implement as CSS data attributes: <div className='office-plant' data-vibe={vibe}>. In Office.css: [data-vibe='crushing'] .office-plant ellipse { filter: brightness(1.2) saturate(1.3); transition: filter 1s ease; } [data-vibe='on-fire'] .office-plant ellipse { fill: #d97706; transition: fill 2s ease; } [data-vibe='slow-day'] .office-plant { transform: rotate(2deg); transition: transform 1.5s ease; }. WHY: The window already changes with time-of-day, the whiteboard with vibe. Plants are the only set of props that never respond to anything. A wilting plant on 'on-fire' day is subliminal storytelling — you feel the office is under stress even before you read the error badge. A blooming plant during 'crushing' is a micro-celebration that doesn't require any user attention.

ideas.title: Footer Quote Click-to-Cycle — Bloberto Has More to Say
ideas.description: The footer renders a hard-coded per-vibe quote in a small element (e.g., "productivity charts are going vertical. don't look down."). These are locked to a static object inside the JSX — they don't pull from VIBE_QUOTES at all. Meanwhile VIBE_QUOTES has 4-5 entries per vibe key in quotes.js. Fix: import VIBE_QUOTES into App.jsx (it's already imported in Office.jsx, just needs App). Add state: const [footerQuoteIdx, setFooterQuoteIdx] = useState(0). When teamVibe.key changes, reset the index to 0 (useEffect dep on teamVibe.key). Replace the static footer span with: setFooterQuoteIdx(i => (i + 1) % VIBE_QUOTES[teamVibe.key].length)} title='Click for another take' role='button' tabIndex={0}>“{VIBE_QUOTES[teamVibe.key]?.[footerQuoteIdx] ?? 'if it compiles, ship it.'}” ↻. CSS: .footer-tagline--clickable { cursor: pointer; transition: opacity 0.15s ease; } .footer-tagline--clickable:hover { opacity: 0.7; } .footer-cycle-hint { font-size: 0.65em; opacity: 0.4; margin-left: 3px; }. WHY: The footer is prime real estate for character voice. Right now Bloberto gets one line per vibe per session. By making it clickable, you invite repeat visits — people click to hear what else he has to say. The 'if it compiles, ship it' quote is good. 'I'm going to need a status update on the status update' is great. Locking Bloberto to one quote per vibe is a waste of VIBE_QUOTES' best material.

ideas.title: Dashboard Section Collapse — Power Users Don't Need Everything at Once
ideas.description:
The dashboard renders two sections in fixed order: 'Active Workers' (with grid) and 'Activity Log' (with filter tabs). Both are always fully expanded. A manager who only cares about the activity stream has to scroll past the entire workers grid. A manager who just checked the log doesn't need it re-expanded next visit. Fix: add collapse state for each section with localStorage persistence. const [workersCollapsed, setWorkersCollapsed] = useState(() => localStorage.getItem('bloberto-workers-collapsed') === 'true'). Add a toggle button to .section-header for each section: . Wrap the workers grid in:
...
. Same pattern for activity log. CSS: .section-collapse-btn { background: none; border: none; color: var(--text-muted); cursor: pointer; font-size: 0.75rem; padding: 4px 6px; border-radius: 4px; transition: color 0.15s; } .section-collapse-btn:hover { color: var(--text-primary); }. When workersCollapsed, show a one-line summary instead:
{active} working · {idle} idle{errorCount > 0 ? ` · ⚠️ ${errorCount} error` : ''}
. WHY: Power users have workflows. A DevOps person watching for errors wants the log front-and-center, not below 6 worker cards. A PM doing a morning standup wants to see the worker grid without scrolling. Collapse + localStorage means the dashboard remembers how you use it. This is the feature that turns occasional visitors into daily users.
